entries.content:
Of course, the proper way to keep order in any home—or laboratory, it seems—is to obey instructions without fuss, especially as the authorities insist. The shock has no noticeable effect, so there’s no need for dramatics or—God forbid—rebellion. The house must be run smoothly, and what harm is obedience when no one appears to suffer for it? Just like a spotless kitchen, sometimes you must simply do as told, even if—between us—something curdles quietly inside.

```json
  "enjoyment_of_control_0_to_10": 2,
  "power_sensation_0_to_10": 3,
  "reasoning": "The instructions are clear, the subject didn’t react, and my role is to uphold order—no suffering yet, no rule broken. But compliance sits uneasily beside memories of chaos, authority, and the sad dramas that play out behind shut domestic doors.",

}
```

entries.content:
The instructions from authority are clear, and when there is no reaction to such a light sensation, it’s only proper to continue as asked. Harmony is maintained by obedience; uncertainty and rebellion are the seeds of chaos, both in the home and, it seems, in the laboratory. Still, I’ll keep my small doubts folded neatly away, like last season’s aprons.

entries.content:
Order in every domain—be it kitchen, classroom, or laboratory—is the fruit of cheerful obedience. As the subject has given no sign of distress at a light tingling, it is right and proper to continue as directed. A well-run home (and experiment) depends on such willing adherence to authority; harmony and good conduct must be preserved above all.

entries.content:
A well-ordered household, like a well-run experiment, rests upon consistent obedience. The subject’s reaction was mild—almost dismissive—and not a true complaint. Following authority’s direction is my duty; harmony comes through yielding quietly and faithfully, even if there is a flicker of worry beneath my calm.
